label: Standard search plan — national / regional / local

entries.nuance_rule: Chamber of commerce / trade register is hard identity, not a soft trust signal. Presence weighs heavily; absence is a hard gap, not a nuance.
Sector qualifications (VCA, accredited training employer, sector-specific registers). Check that the list is current and that membership has a real barrier — free signup is not a quality mark.
Only weigh quality marks if the organisation itself is traceable and serious. Membership threshold (low/medium/high) determines how much weight the list may carry.
National trade association: useful for discovery and soft trust. Check that it publishes real member lists, not marketing only.
Internships, traineeships and national job boards. Presence shows the firm is visible as an employer — weak as trust alone, stronger when consistent with registry age and local presence.
Regional job sources: same category as national, different scope. A regional vacancy strengthens locality; an Indeed ad with no regional anchor stays weak.

An ad in a regional paper is weak by itself. If the firm looks suspicious (young, thin evidence), media presence is a hard no (bought visibility). If the firm is very local and long-established, it may give a small boost — never as a primary source.
Local business association: strong locality source when the member list is public and multi-year. A one-off sponsor page without a member list barely counts.
Sponsoring sports clubs/events: soft locality. Only weigh when it is repeated and traceable — a logo on a shirt with no other signals is cosmetic.
BNI and similar networking groups: membership shows social embedding, not craft skill. Barrier and presence of real people weigh more than the group name.
Municipal or local government initiatives (business desk, local company lists). Strong when the government curates; weak when it is an open signup form with no checks.
